1381:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Wayne A. Thorp, CEO, BetterInvesting

June 12, 2026



Vanessa A. Countryman
Secretary
U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission
100 F Street, NE
Washington, DC 20549-1090


Re: File No. S7-2026-15
Proposed Amendments to Permit Optional Semiannual Reporting by Public Companies
Release Nos. 33-11414; 34-105368; 39-2563; IC-36140


Dear Ms. Countryman:

BetterInvesting is a 75-year-old nonprofit investment education organization serving
approximately 22,000 paying members across the United States. Our mission is to equip
everyday investors to invest wisely and independently. Our members are retail investors. Most
are fundamental, long-term stock pickers who read 10-Q filings.

BetterInvesting opposes the proposed amendments to Exchange Act Rules 13a-13 and 15d-13
that would permit reporting companies to elect filing semiannual reports on new Form 10-S in
lieu of quarterly reports on Form 10-Q.

The proposal would cut in half the number of reviewed, certified, liability-bearing interim
financial reports the U.S. disclosure regime produces each year. Voluntary earnings releases
would fill the gap. Voluntary earnings releases do not carry interim auditor review,
Sarbanes‑Oxley Section 302 certification, or Section 18 liability in the same form as a periodic
report. The empirical literature on this exact question, including work the proposing release
relies on, indicates that the trade would widen information asymmetry, raise the cost of equity
capital, fail to deliver the long-term investment benefits the Commission asserts, and fall hardest
on retail investors. The Commission already took comment on this question in 2018 and
convened a roundtable in 2019.1 It declined to act then. The proposing release does not explain
what has changed in the empirical or investor protection landscape since 2018 to justify
revisiting the question on different terms now.




1
 Request for Comment on Earnings Releases and Quarterly Reports, Release No. 33-10588; 34-
84842; File No. S7-26-18 (Dec. 18, 2018); Roundtable on Short-Term/Long-Term Management
of Public Companies, Our Periodic Reporting System and Regulatory Requirements, U.S.
Securities and Exchange Commission (July 18, 2019).
 1. The proposal would widen information asymmetry between retail and institutional
investors and reduce the legal weight of interim disclosure.

Institutional investors do not depend on the 10-Q the way retail investors do. They have access to
alternative data feeds, credit card panels, satellite imagery, channel checks, expert networks, and
direct corporate access. Retail investors rely on standardized, periodic, reviewed filings. When
companies stop filing quarterly, institutions retain their information edge. Retail investors would
lose half of the standardized data they have today.

The Commission’s own published data establish how unevenly this loss would be distributed.
Senate testimony delivered by the Director of the Division of Trading and Markets in 2015,
drawing on September 2013 ownership data, reported median individual ownership of 89.1
percent for exchange-listed companies with market capitalizations under $50 million, 80.1
percent for companies between $51 million and $100 million, and 68.7 percent for companies
between $101 million and $250 million.2 Institutional ownership became the majority only at
market capitalizations above $500 million. Institutional ownership of smaller companies has
trended upward since the data were collected, primarily through passive index fund growth, but
the relative concentration of retail investors at smaller market capitalizations remains a durable
empirical relationship, and the Commission has not published an updated comprehensive
breakdown contradicting these figures. The proposal, as drafted, permits the largest practicable
reductions in disclosure in precisely the segment of the market where, by the Commission’s own
data, retail investors are the dominant owners.

This is not a theoretical concern. Fu, Kraft, and Zhang examined a comparable natural
experiment using U.S. firms that transitioned between annual, semiannual, and quarterly
reporting between 1951 and 1973. They found that higher reporting frequency reduces
information asymmetry and reduces the cost of equity capital.3 Pozen, Nallareddy, and Rajgopal,
in research supported by the CFA Institute Research Foundation, report that UK firms
voluntarily issuing quarterly reports before the 2007 mandate had lower bid-ask spreads and
higher share turnover than firms that did not.4 Both findings identify the same channel through




2
  Stephen Luparello, Director, Division of Trading and Markets, Testimony on “Venture
Exchanges and Small-Cap Companies,” Before the United States Senate Subcommittee on
Securities, Insurance, and Investment, Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs
(March 10, 2015), Table 1.
3
  Renhui Fu, Arthur Kraft & Huai Zhang, Financial Reporting Frequency, Information
Asymmetry, and the Cost of Equity, 54 Journal of Accounting and Economics 132 (2012).
4
  Robert C. Pozen, Suresh Nallareddy & Shivaram Rajgopal, Impact of Reporting Frequency on
UK Public Companies, CFA Institute Research Foundation (March 2017). The bid-ask spread
and turnover finding for voluntary quarterly reporters is reported at page 4, note 2, citing
Cuijpers and Peek (2010).
 which retail investors benefit from quarterly reporting today, and the same channel that would be
weakened by the proposed rule.

The proposing release argues that voluntary earnings releases will fill any gap left by eliminating
two of four interim filings per year. This conflates information flow with legal protection. Form
10-Q financial statements are subject to interim review under AS 4105. The 10-Q carries CEO
and CFO certifications under Section 302 of the Sarbanes‑Oxley Act. It triggers liability under
Section 18 of the Securities Exchange Act. The proposed Form 10-S would retain those
standards for the semiannual period but would not provide them for the two interim periods that
10-Qs cover today. Voluntary earnings releases furnished under Item 2.02 of Form 8-K do not
carry equivalent legal protections; they are furnished rather than filed precisely to limit issuer
liability.

The Commission would be replacing two reviewed, certified, liability-bearing interim filings per
year with corporate communications that carry materially weaker legal protections. Retail
investors are the constituency most dependent on those protections, and as the Commission’s
own data show, they hold the most stock in the companies the proposing release identifies as the
principal intended beneficiaries of the new regime.

2. The empirical record on reporting frequency does not support the proposal.

The Commission’s premise, that less frequent reporting will reduce managerial short-termism
(i.e., the corporate myopia hypothesis) and improve long-term outcomes, is testable, and the
literature has tested it. The leading U.S. paper cited in support of the Commission’s premise is
Kraft, Vashishtha, and Venkatachalam, which finds that increased reporting frequency among
U.S. firms between 1950 and 1970 was associated with reduced corporate capital investment,
which the authors interpret as evidence of managerial myopia.5 Three points limit the weight this
finding should carry in this rulemaking, and the more recent natural-experiment evidence cuts
uniformly against the proposal.

First, the dependent variable is capital investment by the firm. The Commission’s statutory
mandate is investor protection, not capital expenditure levels. A decline in corporate capital
investment associated with more frequent reporting is not the same as a decline in long-term
shareholder welfare. The myopia hypothesis assumes that the forgone investments would have
been positive net present value (NPV) projects whose abandonment harms shareholders. The
literature does not establish that. The forgone investments may have included negative-NPV
projects whose abandonment benefits shareholders, or positive-NPV projects whose
abandonment harms shareholders, or some mix. Until the literature distinguishes between these



5
Arthur G. Kraft, Rahul Vashishtha & Mohan Venkatachalam, Frequent Financial Reporting and
Managerial Myopia, 93 The Accounting Review 249 (2018).
 cases, the myopia channel does not justify reducing investor disclosure on the basis of preserving
issuer capital expenditure levels.

Second, the more recent natural experiment cuts the other way. Pozen, Nallareddy, and Rajgopal
exploited the United Kingdom’s adoption of mandatory quarterly reporting in 2007 and its repeal
in 2014.6 They found no statistically significant differences in capital expenditures, research and
development, or net property, plant, and equipment between mandatory switchers and voluntary
adopters in the years after 2007. They reached the same finding for stoppers compared to
continuers after 2014. The frequency of financial reporting did not materially affect corporate
investment levels in either direction. The myopia channel that the Commission’s proposing
release relies on does not replicate in the modern UK natural experiment.

Third, the UK experience produced findings that directly contradict the proposal’s premises
about investor outcomes. The introduction of mandatory quarterly reporting in 2007 was
associated with a 21.5 percent increase in the average number of analysts following mandatory
switchers, from 2.79 to 3.39, and a 26.5 percent increase for voluntary adopters, from 3.77 to
4.78. Analyst forecast accuracy improved more for mandatory switchers than for voluntary
adopters. When the mandate was repealed in 2014, companies that stopped reporting quarterly
experienced a statistically significant decline in analyst coverage.7 Each of these findings
undercuts the rationale for the proposed rule. Quarterly reporting brought analysts to the market.
Less frequent reporting drove them away.

Fourth, when the UK gave companies the option to drop quarterly reporting in 2014, fewer than
10 percent of UK public companies in the Pozen study sample (45 of 471) did so by the end of
2015.8 More than 90 percent continued quarterly reporting even with no requirement to do so.
The proposing release cites the UK repeal as a policy precedent for this rulemaking. The release
does not mention the 90-plus percent retention rate. The companies most exposed to the alleged
costs of quarterly reporting voted with their filings to keep it.

BetterInvesting acknowledges that Pozen, Nallareddy, and Rajgopal, while finding no support
for the move from quarterly to semiannual reporting, do recommend reform of the existing
quarterly reporting framework. They favor streamlined quarterly reporting with sector-specific
guidelines and key performance indicators rather than the current 10-Q format. The
Commission’s proposal addresses reporting frequency, not reporting content. The Pozen analysis
supports the latter, not the former. If the Commission is interested in reducing the compliance
burden of the current 10-Q while preserving its investor protection function, the path forward is
content reform, not frequency reduction. The two are not interchangeable, and the empirical
record cited above does not support treating them as such.


6
  Pozen, Nallareddy & Rajgopal, supra note 4.
7
  Id.
8
  Id.
 The empirical finding most directly relevant to the Commission’s investor protection mandate,
that higher reporting frequency reduces information asymmetry and lowers the cost of equity,
comes from the same historical natural experiment Kraft, Vashishtha, and Venkatachalam use
and rests on the same underlying data.9 The Commission cannot selectively rely on one half of
that older literature while ignoring the other half and the more recent UK evidence that
uniformly runs counter to the proposal.

The proposing release also cites a 2019 Nasdaq survey in which 75 percent of 183 responding
listed companies indicated that they or their investors would benefit from semiannual reporting.
That is a survey of issuers, not investors. Asking companies what they think investors want is not
the same as asking investors.

Investor-side survey evidence on this question exists. In 2019, the CFA Institute surveyed 28,204
chartered financial analysts worldwide and reported the results.10 Among the 768 respondents, 91
percent reported that quarterly reports contain incremental financial information beyond what
earnings releases provide, 75 percent reported that the incremental information in a quarterly
report can affect their views about a company, and 72 percent rejected the “supplemental
approach” under consideration at the time that would have permitted earnings releases to
substitute for the financial content of a Form 10-Q. Based on that member survey, the CFA
Institute formally opposed any move to semiannual reporting. The Council of Institutional
Investors, representing pension funds and other long-term institutional investors with member
assets of approximately $4 trillion, separately characterized quarterly reports as “a critical
element of the robust disclosure infrastructure that is so important for investors” and warned that
less frequent reporting “would likely lead to greater share price volatility, and more intense
investor focus on short-term share price fluctuations.”11 This investor-side record speaks to the
same question the Commission is now revisiting and runs against any move to semiannual
reporting.

3. Comparability and trend analysis across companies would degrade.

If some companies elect semiannual reporting while others continue quarterly reporting, retail
investors face a fragmented disclosure landscape. Comparing two competitors in the same
industry becomes harder when one reports quarterly and the other twice a year. Sector analysis,



9
 Fu, Kraft & Zhang, supra note 3.
10
   CFA Institute, The Case for Quarterly and Environmental, Social, and Governance Reporting
(2019). The survey was administered to a random sample of 28,204 CFA Institute charterholders
employed as quantitative analysts or portfolio managers from February 27 to March 10, 2019,
with 768 useable responses, a 3 percent response rate, and a margin of error of plus or minus 3.5
percentage points at a 95 percent confidence level.
11
   Council of Institutional Investors, Comment Letter to the U.S. Securities and Exchange
Commission on Earnings Releases and Quarterly Reports, File No. S7-26-18 (2019).
 peer benchmarking, and the screening tools that retail investors and the educational organizations
that serve them rely on all depend on consistent reporting intervals.

This concern compounds for investors using fundamental analysis methodologies that rely on
multi-period data. BetterInvesting teaches the Stock Selection Guide, which requires investors to
evaluate trends in sales growth, EPS growth, pre-tax profit margins, return on equity, and debt
levels across multiple periods. Eight quarters of data per two-year window is the foundation of
credible trend analysis. Four data points per two years materially weaken the statistical
foundation for the kind of patient, fundamental analysis that long-term retail investing depends
on.

4. The proposed Regulation S-X amendments would permit materially stale financial
statements in primary offerings.

The proposing release indicates that Regulation S-X would be amended to address the age of
financial statements for semiannual filers, including changes intended to prevent semiannual
filers’ financial statements from being deemed stale under existing rules built around a quarterly
framework. The fact sheet describes this as a simplification and consolidation of the staleness
rules.

The age-of-financial-statements rules in Regulation S-X exist to protect investors who purchase
newly issued securities. A retail investor buying shares in a follow-on offering or an IPO is
entitled to make that decision based on reasonably current financial information.

If the Commission relaxes the staleness window to accommodate semiannual filers, retail
investors purchasing in primary offerings from those filers will be pricing the offering off
financial statements up to seven months old at the time of issuance. That is a substantively
weaker level of disclosure than what investors in primary offerings receive today. The
Commission should not modify the Regulation S-X staleness framework in a way that converts
financial statements that are stale by current investor protection standards into financial
statements that are no longer stale by definition.

Recommendations

   1. The Commission should not adopt this proposal. The empirical record, including the
      Commission’s own data on retail ownership concentration in smaller companies and the
      UK natural experiment that the proposing release cites as policy precedent, does not
      support the proposed change. The size-tiered alternative that some commenters may
      propose, under which the semiannual election would be limited to smaller filers, would
      compound the harm to retail investors rather than mitigate it: the Commission’s data
      establish that retail ownership is most concentrated at smaller companies, and the same
      Pozen, Nallareddy, and Rajgopal research cited above explicitly warns against size-tiered
      reporting cadence on the grounds that it would reduce liquidity and raise the cost of
           capital for smaller companies.12 A size threshold is not a remedy. The cleanest course is
          to preserve the existing framework.

      2. If the Commission proceeds with this rulemaking notwithstanding the empirical record, it
         should retain the current Regulation S-X staleness framework for primary offerings. Any
         company conducting a registered offering should be required to include financial
         information no older than that required of a quarterly filer, regardless of the issuer’s
         elected interim reporting cadence. The trade-off between issuer compliance burden and
         ongoing periodic reporting differs from the trade-off between issuer compliance burden
         and the freshness of information available to investors at the time of a primary offering.
         The Commission should not bundle them.

Quarterly reporting is not an administrative burden the Commission should be eager to lift. It is
the architecture that has given American retail investors enough standardized, certified
information to participate in U.S. capital markets on something approaching equal footing with
sophisticated investors for more than half a century. The empirical record, including the
Commission’s own data, indicates that weakening that architecture would impose its largest
costs on the constituency the Commission exists to protect. BetterInvesting urges the
Commission not to do so.

Thank you for the opportunity to comment. I would welcome the chance to discuss these
concerns with you or your staff at any time.



Sincerely,




Wayne A. Thorp, CFA
CEO
BetterInvesting
570 Kirts Blvd., Suite 221
Troy, MI 48084

cc:       Office of the Investor Advocate, U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission

     Pozen, Nallareddy & Rajgopal, supra note 4.

Re: Comment Letter in Support of Proposed Amendments to Allow Optional Semiannual
Reporting

Release Nos. 33-11414; 34-105368; IC-36140 (May 5, 2026)
File No. S7-2026-15

Dear Commissioners and Staff:

         The Investor Choice Advocates Network (“ICAN”) appreciates the opportunity to
comment on the Securities and Exchange Commission’s proposed rule to allow public companies
to file semiannual reports on new Form 10-S in lieu of quarterly reports on Form 10-Q (“Proposed
Rule”).1 ICAN is a nonprofit public interest law firm and advocacy organization dedicated to
expanding access to capital markets and protecting the fundamental right of investors to make their
own informed financial decisions.
         ICAN supports the Proposed Rule and its expansion of investor choice. The ability of
investors to freely choose where to invest their capital is a cornerstone of efficient markets, and
the Proposed Rule advances that principle by giving investors and companies alike the ability to
choose a reporting cadence that best reflects their needs. Critics argue that less frequent mandatory
reporting harms retail investors by reducing available information. As discussed below, this
concern, while sincere, gives investors too little credit and the government too much, for we share
the critics’ commitment to keeping retail investors informed and simply trust that investors, given
the choice, will demand the information they need. The market, not regulators, is best positioned
to determine the optimal level of reporting frequency.

1. The Proposed Rule Expands Investor Choice Without Reducing Anyone’s Options
       A threshold point frequently overlooked by critics is that the Proposed Rule takes nothing
away from any investor. Any company that wishes to continue filing quarterly reports on Form
10-Q may continue to do so. Any investor who values frequent disclosure may simply choose to



1
 Proposed Rule: Semiannual Reporting, Release Nos. 33-11414; 34-105368; IC-36140, at 2 (May 5,
2026), available at https://www.sec.gov/files/rules/proposed/2026/33-11414.pdf.
            INVESTOR CHOICE ADVOCATES NETWORK
453 S. Spring Street, Suite 400 • Los Angeles, CA 90013 • www.icanlaw.org
invest in quarterly-filing companies. The Proposed Rule is strictly additive: it creates a new option
for companies and investors, not a mandate.
        This is the essence of investor choice. Markets function best when participants can sort
themselves by preference. Not every investor wants the same thing: some want quarterly
checkpoints, while others prefer companies freed from the pressure of quarterly targets. The
Proposed Rule enables both groups to act on their preferences simultaneously. Denying companies
the option to report semiannually forecloses choices for investors who might prefer companies
freed from the expense and short-termism that quarterly earnings guidance can produce.
        Critics who favor mandatory quarterly reporting implicitly assume that market discipline
is insufficient to protect investors who prefer more frequent disclosure. That assumption is
unwarranted. A company that elects semiannual reporting over the objections of its shareholder
base will face swift market consequences: reduced trading volume, diminished analyst coverage,
and a higher cost of capital. Institutional and retail investors alike can, and will, express their
preference through their investment decisions, avoiding semiannual filers in favor of companies
that report quarterly.
        A less liquid stock means fewer analysts, costlier fundraising, and a weaker position in
capital transactions and hiring. The market creates these pressures on its own, without any
regulatory mandate. As one prominent financial commentator noted shortly after the Proposed
Rule was released, large established companies are unlikely to shift to semiannual reporting
precisely because their investors expect quarterly updates and the companies understand the
market cost of disappointing that expectation.2 The Commission need not mandate what the market
will effectively require. ICAN’s mission is to expand market access and protect investor autonomy.
A rule that gives investors more choices is precisely the kind of market-expanding reform we
support.
        The mandatory quarterly reporting requirement also implicates a deeper structural concern.
Congress designed the Exchange Act's periodic reporting framework as a disclosure regime — one
intended to ensure that investors receive the information they need to make their own investment
decisions, not to restrict which investment options are available to them in the first place. A merits-
based regulator substitutes the government's judgment for the investor's by prohibiting access to
investments the regulator deems insufficiently attractive; a disclosure-based regulator ensures that
investors receive full and accurate information and then trusts them to decide. The existing
mandatory quarterly reporting rule, to the extent it bars investors from accessing public companies
that would choose to report semiannually, functions as the former: it withholds market access not
because any required disclosure has been omitted, but because a regulator has deemed quarterly
reporting to be a precondition for participation. The Proposed Rule corrects this structural drift by
returning the reporting-frequency decision to companies and investors, consistent with the




2
 Matt Levine, Sometimes Andrew Left Was Right, Bloomberg Opinion (May 18, 2026), available at
https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/newsletters/2026-05-18/sometimes-andrew-left-was-right.
            INVESTOR CHOICE ADVOCATES NETWORK
453 S. Spring Street, Suite 400 • Los Angeles, CA 90013 • www.icanlaw.org
Exchange Act's foundational commitment to informed investor choice over government-imposed
merit judgments.3

2. The “Less Information” Criticism Does Not Withstand Scrutiny
       The most common objection to the Proposed Rule is that quarterly reports provide investors
with more information, and therefore their elimination would harm retail investors. This concern
deserves a direct response.
        First, switching to semiannual reporting does not give companies a pass on disclosing
material news. Under Form 8-K, companies are already required to report significant
developments promptly as they occur, such as earnings surprises, executive changes, mergers,
regulatory actions, and dozens of other triggering events, and the Proposed Rule leaves those
obligations entirely intact. Semiannual filers will continue to report material developments as they
arise. The Proposed Rule reduces the frequency of scheduled interim reports; it does not create a
disclosure blackout.
        Second, the “more information is always better” argument confuses quantity with quality.
As Harvard Law Professor Hal Scott, Director of the Committee on Capital Markets Regulation,
has observed, the quarterly reporting regime is closely intertwined with the practice of issuing
quarterly earnings guidance, a practice widely cited as a driver of “short-termism,” pushing
management to optimize for meeting three-month earnings estimates rather than long-term value
creation.4 Academic research reinforces this concern: empirical studies have documented that
increased reporting frequency is associated with a meaningful decline in corporate investment and
an increase in real earnings management—findings consistent with the short-termism hypothesis.5
A leading comment letter submitted to this proceeding by Professor Stephen Bainbridge of UCLA

between disclosure-based and merits-based securities regulation and noting that Congress, in establishing
the federal securities regulatory framework, deliberately rejected merit regulation in favor of a disclosure-
based approach that leaves investment decisions to investors).
4

5

that EU firms mandated to switch from semiannual to quarterly reporting showed increased real earnings

(2018). Frequent financial reporting and managerial myopia. The Accounting Review, 93(2), 249–275,

following increased reporting frequency, consistent with managerial myopia); Gigler, F., Kanodia, C.,

School of Law similarly concludes that mandatory quarterly reporting rests on weak theoretical
foundations, because market forces, anti-fraud liability, and event-driven disclosure requirements
can deliver adequate investor information without a rigid reporting mandate.6 Quarterly reports
that principally confirm or miss guidance targets may generate less genuinely useful information
for long-term investors than less frequent but more substantive disclosures focused on business
strategy and multi-year performance.
        Third, and most importantly, the international evidence refutes the prediction that
eliminating mandatory quarterly reporting will leave investors in the dark. The United Kingdom
repealed its quarterly reporting mandate in 2014. The European Union did the same in 2013,
having introduced a quarterly requirement only in 2004.7 In both jurisdictions, many public
companies continue to report quarterly on a voluntary basis, exactly what market forces would
predict.8 Investors who want quarterly reports from UK and EU companies can get them, because
companies understand that voluntarily providing information investors value serves their own
interests. The same dynamic will operate in U.S. markets under the Proposed Rule.

3. Reducing Compliance Costs Strengthens U.S. Public Markets
       Beyond investor choice, the Proposed Rule serves a compelling public-interest goal:
reducing the regulatory burden that has contributed to the declining number of publicly traded
companies in the United States and the lengthening of private holding periods. The Commission’s
own economic analysis estimates that filing three quarterly reports per year imposes approximately
$330,0009 in annual direct compliance costs per issuer before accounting for the cost of the Form
10-K, costs the SEC expects will be meaningfully reduced under the Proposed Rule, with the
savings reinvested in business operations, growth, and long-term strategy.10
       For large, established companies, this cost is manageable. For newly public companies,
smaller issuers, and companies in capital-intensive industries, it is a significant diversion of
resources from research and development, hiring, and capital investment. The burden of quarterly

compliance is frequently cited as a factor in the decision of growth-stage companies to remain
private longer, a concern the Proposed Rule directly addresses, as reduced compliance costs are
expected to encourage more private companies to enter and remain in the public markets.11 The
Proposed Rule addresses this dynamic directly, making U.S. public markets more accessible and
competitive.
       New companies often have little of substance to report on a quarterly basis beyond
continued investment of capital in early-stage development activities.12 Requiring these companies
to produce costly quarterly reports primarily to confirm that their business plan has not materially
changed is an inefficient use of both company resources and Commission staff time. The Proposed
Rule appropriately gives these companies the option to report semiannually, preserving capital for
productive use while maintaining annual and event-driven disclosures.
        The Proposed Rule also complements the Commission’s concurrent registered offering
reform proposal (Release No. 33-11418), which would dramatically expand Form S-3 eligibility
to early-stage issuers regardless of public float or exchange-listing status—precisely the companies
that benefit most from semiannual reporting.13 For these companies, investor valuations are driven
primarily by milestone achievement, not incremental revenues or earnings, and those milestones
are already captured in Form 8-K filings. Quarterly profit and loss reports and balance sheet
changes provide little additional analytical value, and while cash flow remains a legitimate
concern, semiannual disclosure supplemented by event-driven Form 8-K reporting adequately
addresses it. Together, semiannual reporting and expanded Form S-3 access create a coherent,
lower-friction pathway for early-stage issuers to raise capital while maintaining disclosure
standards appropriate to their lifecycle.
        Critics may counter that pairing expanded capital-raising access with reduced reporting
frequency creates an information gap precisely when investors are most vulnerable, particularly
for early-stage companies spending large amounts of cash between semiannual filings. Existing
law already addresses this concern: material impairments (Item 2.06), covenant defaults and debt
acceleration (Item 2.04), and the creation of material financial obligations (Item 2.03) all trigger
Form 8-K disclosure within four business days.14 Moreover, each Form S-3 shelf offering
incorporates the company’s most recent Exchange Act filings by reference, supplementing rather

than undermining the semiannual disclosure calendar. The federal securities laws provide a
further layer of protection that quarterly reporting cannot replicate.16 Investors who acquire
securities in a registered offering have private rights of action under Securities Act Sections 11
and 12(a)(2) for material misstatements or omissions, and underwriters, accountants, directors, and
other signatories face strict liability for such misstatements. This incentivizes all parties to ensure
the accuracy and completeness of disclosures at the time of each offering regardless of when
interim reports are due.17

4. The Proposed Rule Preserves Meaningful Disclosure Standards
        ICAN also wishes to highlight that the Proposed Rule is carefully designed to preserve the
quality of disclosure for companies that elect semiannual reporting. New Form 10-S would require
the same narrative disclosures and financial information currently required by Form 10-Q,
covering a six-month fiscal period rather than a quarter.18 Auditor review of semiannual financial
statements would remain required. The filing deadlines for Form 10-S align with those for Form
10-Q (40 or 45 days depending on filer status).19 The Proposed Rule offers a genuine middle path:
a structured, auditor-reviewed semiannual report, not a regulatory free-for-all.

Conclusion
        The Proposed Rule represents a thoughtful, market-respecting reform that expands investor
choice, reduces unnecessary compliance burdens, and aligns U.S. reporting practices with
international standards. The concerns raised by critics, while sincere, give investors too little credit
and the government too much. ICAN trusts that investors can and will make those judgments for
themselves. ICAN supports the Commission in finalizing the Proposed Rule for the reasons set
forth above.

Thank you.

139:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: David Bolling Wells

SEC Comment Letter on Optional 6 Month Reporting Rule

As a former VP of Financial Planning & Analysis and then CFO of high growth company Netflix, I went through more than 60 quarterly reporting cycles with high stakes calls on both the creation and stress testing of content accounting that was being created for a new industry and guidance setting for a high profile company in a dynamic marketplace. Up close and personal. Our stock saw dramatic volatility, some of which we had trouble explaining. I went on to serve on the pre-public Audit and Risk Committee and then Chair of the Board of fintech company Wise (formerly Transferwise) which was the largest public listing on the LSE. Wise just switched on 5/11/26 to being a Nasdaq US primary listing after reporting every 6 months as a UK company in the first roughly 5 years of our public life. I have seen the cons and merits of the traditional 4 quarter cycle and the 6 month formal cycle with a "trading update" interim release.

There are good arguments for an optional 6 month opt in so I understand why many frustrated CFO and finance teams would propose and argue for it.

I am here to comment that on balance, I think the 4 quarter cycle is the best compromise and underpins the United States' superior trust (and value) position in global capital markets. The four quarter model drives discipline and rigor, and provides long investors with a reasonable 4 times-a-year check in on a company's progress on its long term strategic and financial goals. It also is a foundational element of "democratic capital markets," attempting to even the playing field for individual investors versus large institutions that can command regular check-ins with management.

Yes, the 4 quarter cycle is grinding, demanding, and "can" lead to overly short term financial focus but that is up to a company's financial leadership and investor relations to shape and manage. If the 6 month reporting opt in is enacted, what will happen is that a small minority of companies in industries that do match a twice-a-year reporting will be overjoyed and adopt it. But then, another group will adopt and this will become a mixed bag of lesser financially disciplined companies and the market will be left to sift through this and apply discounted value or higher risk. Fraud, slower impairments, increased insider knowledge value, and bad strategy living longer will all be the bogies of the slower reporting cycle.

I urge the SEC to make decisions that keep our US capital markets the envy of the world and stay with the current model. Don't confuse the need for securities litigation reform (loser pays legal fees) with changes, even optional, to the reporting cycle.

Sincerely,

David Wells
Chair WSE, Lead Independent HIMS, former CFO NFLX
13:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Skyler Mathis

From a leadership and cost-management standpoint, the initial concept behind this proposal appears well-intentioned and, at a high level, may seem attractive. However, when viewed through the lens of long-term operational effectiveness, reporting quality, and investor confidence, there are significant risks that warrant careful consideration.

One key concern is the likely reduction in dedicated external and SEC reporting teams within organizations. While this may appear to lower fixed costs, it could ultimately lead many companies to rely more heavily on recurring contractor support. From a leadership perspective, this model introduces inefficiencies and risk. A revolving cast of contractors often lacks deep institutional knowledge, which is critical for producing high-quality, consistent, and accurate external reporting. Over time, this could negatively impact the reliability of disclosures and increase risk for the company and its participants. Leadership teams rely on frequent and consistent reporting not only to meet regulatory requirements, but also to reinforce transparency and trust with investors. Reducing reporting frequency may unintentionally weaken that relationship and limit investors' ability to make informed decisions.

Additionally, reporting less frequently would necessitate a significant overhaul of interim reporting requirements. To compensate for reduced reporting frequency, companies would likely need to disclose more detailed information during interim periods. This could increase complexity rather than reduce burden, potentially undermining the proposal's objective. The SEC would also need to develop a much more robust interim reporting standard to ensure investors remain adequately informed about a company's financial and operational performance on a semi-annual basis.

Ultimately, while the proposal reflects thoughtful intent, leadership must evaluate whether it truly aligns with the broader responsibilities that come with being a public company. Although quarterly reporting may limit flexibility, it is an understood and accepted aspect of accessing public capital. Increased reporting, monitoring, and scrutiny are fundamental to protecting investors and maintaining confidence in the capital markets. From a leadership perspective, those expectations should not only be accepted — but embraced — as part of responsible corporate governance.
