1845:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Tracy Ayers, CPA, Cerex Advisory

June 7, 2026


Vanessa A. Countryman, Secretary
Securities and Exchange Commission
100 F Street NE
Washington, DC 20549-1090


Re: File No. S7-2026-15 — Semiannual Reporting (Proposed Rule)


Dear Secretary Countryman:


I am a Certified Public Accountant with eleven years of practice specializing in
partnership taxation, complex syndicated structures, and high-net-worth client advisory
work. I submit these comments in my individual professional capacity, in response to
the Commission's invitation for public comment on the proposed amendments to allow
optional semiannual interim reporting on new Form 10-S in lieu of quarterly reporting on
Form 10-Q.
I oppose the adoption of the proposed rule as presented. My opposition is not to the
concept of reducing unnecessary compliance burden on reporting companies. It is to
the specific mechanism chosen and to the predictable pattern of who will elect it, and at
whose expense.

I. The Framing of This Proposal as a Burden-Reduction Measure Obscures Its
Investor Protection Costs
The proposing release frames optional semiannual reporting primarily as a compliance
cost reduction for reporting companies. That framing is accurate as far as it goes. What
it does not adequately address is the asymmetric distribution of who absorbs the cost of
that reduction.
Quarterly reporting under Form 10-Q is not merely an accounting exercise. It is one of
the few remaining structural mechanisms through which the investing public, not
institutional analysts with direct management access, not sell-side desks with
proprietary data feeds, obtains standardized, filed, legally accountable financial
information about the companies whose securities they hold.
The Commission asks in its economic analysis whether semiannual reporting would
meaningfully affect the information environment for different classes of investors. The
answer, in my professional judgment, is yes, and not symmetrically. Institutional
 investors have the resources, access, and analytical capacity to construct a reasonable
picture of a company's financial condition from earnings releases, management
guidance, 8-K filings, and alternative data sources. Retail investors largely do not. For
retail participants, the Form 10-Q is the disclosure, not a supplement to it.
Reducing the frequency of that disclosure from three times annually to once does not
eliminate the information gap between institutional and retail investors. It widens it.

II. The "Optional" Structure Will Not Produce Neutral Outcomes
The Commission has characterized the proposal as optional, with each company
electing the reporting frequency that best serves its circumstances and investors. In
theory, this is neutral. In practice, it is not.
The Commission's own analysis identifies smaller, development-stage, and speculative-
growth companies as the likely adopters of semiannual reporting, specifically because
quarterly reporting imposes relatively higher fixed costs on these companies relative to
their size, and because their investors are characterized as focused on non-financial
milestones rather than interim financial results.
This characterization should concern the Commission. Smaller and development-stage
companies are precisely the issuers in which retail investors are most concentrated and
least protected by alternative information sources. These are not the companies with
robust analyst coverage, active investor relations programs, or frequent management
access for large shareholders. They are the companies whose retail shareholders are
most dependent on filed financial disclosures as their primary source of material
information.
An optional rule that predictably concentrates in the issuer segment where retail
investors are most exposed is not a neutral flexibility measure. It is a structural
reduction in the information rights of the investors least positioned to compensate.

III. Earnings Releases Are Not an Adequate Substitute for Filed Quarterly Reports
The Commission acknowledges in the proposing release that companies electing
semiannual reporting may continue to issue quarterly earnings releases, and asks
whether this practice would mitigate investor information concerns. I submit that it would
not, and that this question reflects a conflation of furnished disclosure with filed
disclosure that warrants direct address.
Quarterly earnings releases are furnished, not filed. They are not subject to the same
liability framework as Form 10-Q. A company that furnishes an earnings release and
later files a Form 10-S that tells a materially different story has not violated the same set
of obligations as a company that files a deficient quarterly report. The accountant review
requirement, which currently applies to interim financial statements included in Form 10-
Q under PCAOB AS 4105, does not apply to furnished earnings releases.
Permitting a semiannual filer to satisfy its interim disclosure obligations through
voluntary, unreviewed, furnished releases, while characterizing the total disclosure
 environment as substantially equivalent to quarterly reporting, overstates the investor
protection value of that arrangement. The legal accountability structure is not
equivalent. The quality assurance framework is not equivalent. Framing voluntary
practice as a substitute for mandatory obligation is not a sound basis for rule adoption.
There is a further consequence of reduced disclosure frequency that the proposing
release does not adequately address: the concentration of material nonpublic
information exposure into longer and fewer windows. Under the current quarterly
framework, the period during which insiders possess material information that has not
yet been publicly filed is bounded by a 40 or 45-day reporting deadline. Semiannual
reporting doubles that window by definition. For companies that also cease issuing
quarterly earnings releases upon electing semiannual reporting, the extension of that
window is not merely administrative, it creates a structurally longer period during which
the gap between what insiders know and what the market can price is at its maximum.
The practical consequences include longer mandatory blackout periods, increased
complexity in administering Rule 10b5-1 trading plans, and heightened Regulation FD
exposure at investor conferences and analyst meetings occurring in the months
between filed disclosures. These are not theoretical concerns for practitioners advising
reporting companies. They are the operational reality of a reduced-cadence disclosure
environment, and they fall asymmetrically on retail investors who have no alternative
channel through which to access the information insiders hold.

IV. The Foreign Private Issuer Comparison Does Not Support Generalization
Proponents of this proposal have cited the experience of foreign private issuers, which
are not subject to Form 10-Q requirements and operate on semiannual interim
reporting, as evidence that reduced reporting frequency is compatible with functional
capital markets.
This comparison has significant limitations. Foreign private issuers operate within home-
country regulatory frameworks that in many cases provide structural investor protections
not present in the U.S. system for the issuer categories most likely to elect semiannual
reporting here. The applicable disclosure regimes, liability standards, shareholder rights
structures, and alternative information environments differ materially. Drawing a direct
equivalence between the FPI experience and the likely experience of U.S. domestic
small-cap and development-stage issuers electing Form 10-S does not follow from the
comparative evidence.

V. The Public Trust Dimension
I want to raise a concern that falls outside the Commission's standard cost-benefit
framework but is, in my view, material to the policy judgment here.
Public markets function on the premise that participation is accessible to the public, not
just to participants with the resources, access, and analytical infrastructure to construct
their own information picture independent of mandatory disclosure. The Commission's
periodic reporting requirements are part of the structural infrastructure that supports that
 premise. They are not merely compliance costs. They are part of what makes a public
market public.
At a moment when retail investor participation in public markets is at historically high
levels, and when public confidence in the fairness of those markets is not. The
Commission should be cautious about rule changes that structurally reduce the
information available to the investors least equipped to compensate through private
means. The compliance cost savings to issuers are real. The cost to the information
environment of retail investors is also real, and it falls on a different population than the
one that captures the benefit.
This is not an argument that compliance costs do not matter. It is an argument that the
Commission should weigh that trade-off explicitly, rather than treating investor
protection concerns as adequately addressed by the voluntary continuation of practices
that are structurally and legally inferior to the mandatory disclosures they would replace.

VI. Reduced Interim Controls Cadence in an Era of AI-Assisted Financial
Reporting
The Commission's proposal arrives at a moment when AI-assisted tools are being
deployed at scale across financial reporting functions: in reconciliations, variance
analysis, disclosure drafting, and internal control monitoring. This context is material to
the controls cadence question, and I submit that it weighs against reducing interim
reporting frequency.
Section 302 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act requires CEO and CFO certification of financial
statements and disclosure controls at each interim and annual filing. Under the current
quarterly framework, that certification cycle occurs three times annually before the
annual Form 10-K. It functions not only as a legal accountability mechanism but as a
forcing function: it requires management to affirmatively evaluate the integrity of
financial reporting processes on a regular, defined cadence. Reducing that cadence to
once annually, with a single interim checkpoint, does not eliminate the statutory
obligation. It materially reduces the frequency with which that obligation requires active
management attention.
This matters more, not less, as AI-assisted tools take on larger roles in financial
reporting workflows. The reliability of AI-assisted processes is not static. Model behavior
can degrade between evaluation points through updates, configuration changes, or drift
in the underlying data environment without generating the kind of visible failure signal
that would prompt unscheduled management review. A quarterly certification cadence
creates regular, mandatory human verification checkpoints in a process that is
otherwise increasingly automated. Semiannual reporting removes two of those
checkpoints per year.
This is not a theoretical concern. Documented failures in AI-assisted processes,
including in high-stakes professional contexts, share a common feature: the failure
accumulated between evaluation points, in the intervals where no mandatory human
review was required. Reducing the number of those intervals in financial reporting does
 not reduce the risk of AI-assisted error. It extends the window in which such errors can
propagate undetected before a required certification forces review.
The Commission's proposal does not address this dynamic. The proposing release
does not analyze the interaction between reduced reporting frequency and the
expanding role of automated tools in financial reporting processes. I urge the
Commission to solicit comment specifically on this question before finalizing any rule,
and to consider whether companies electing semiannual reporting should be required to
disclose the extent to which AI-assisted tools are used in financial reporting functions,
and what human verification controls are in place in the absence of the quarterly
certification cadence.

VII. Disclosure Quality, the Transparency Premium, and Foreign Capital
Allocation
The Commission frames this proposal as a competitiveness measure, reducing
compliance burden to encourage more companies to go and remain public, thereby
strengthening U.S. capital markets relative to international peers. I submit that this
framing inverts the actual competitiveness dynamic and that the Commission has not
adequately analyzed the risk that reduced disclosure quality could impair, rather than
enhance, the ability of U.S. public markets to attract and retain foreign institutional
capital.
U.S. public equity markets have historically commanded a valuation premium relative to
comparable international markets. That premium is not solely a function of economic
size or liquidity. It reflects, in part, a disclosure infrastructure: quarterly GAAP financials,
accountant review, SOX certification, and EDGAR accessibility that provides foreign
institutional allocators with a standardized, legally accountable information environment
they cannot replicate through private channels. That infrastructure is part of what makes
U.S. equities a default allocation for global capital, particularly at the institutional level.
The proposal does not eliminate that infrastructure for quarterly filers. But it introduces a
class of domestic issuers for whom that infrastructure is materially reduced and it does
so without any disclosure requirement that would allow a foreign institutional allocator to
identify, at the portfolio level, which of their U.S. equity holdings operate under the
reduced framework. An allocator running a U.S. small-cap sleeve would need to check
the cover page of each issuer's most recent Form 10-K to determine whether that issuer
is subject to quarterly or semiannual reporting obligations. That is not a workable
disclosure environment for cross-border capital allocation at scale.
The Commission's proposing release does not analyze this dynamic. It does not
estimate the share of foreign institutional capital currently allocated to domestic issuers
in the size and stage categories most likely to elect semiannual reporting, nor does it
model the reallocation risk if those issuers are perceived as operating under a
structurally weaker disclosure standard than their international counterparts in
comparable markets. Treasury desks at reporting companies have already identified
this risk directly: the operative concern is whether investors will continue to purchase
securities from issuers whose most recent filed financial statements are six months old
 at the time of a capital markets transaction. That concern is not hypothetical. It is the
question debt and equity underwriters will have to answer for every semiannual filer that
accesses the capital markets.
The Commission should not assume that reducing compliance burden on issuers
translates automatically into stronger markets. If the disclosure reduction causes even a
modest reallocation of foreign institutional capital away from the issuer categories most
likely to elect semiannual reporting, the net effect on capital formation in those
categories may be negative, not positive. That analysis is absent from the proposing
release, and the Commission should conduct it before finalizing any rule.

VIII. Index Standard Fragmentation as a Leading Indicator
The Commission's proposal arrives against a backdrop of accelerating fragmentation in
the standards applied by major index providers to listing eligibility and index inclusion:
fragmentation that is itself producing the kind of retail investor comparability failure the
Commission's disclosure rules exist to prevent. This fragmentation is directly relevant to
the question of what semiannual reporting will mean in practice for investors trying to
understand the composition of the index products they hold.
The recent SpaceX IPO process illustrates the dynamic. When SpaceX and its advisers
sought expedited inclusion in major U.S. equity indexes, index providers responded
differently. Nasdaq amended its Fast Entry rule to allow newly listed large-cap
companies to join the Nasdaq-100 within fifteen trading days of listing; a change that did
not exist before this transaction and was adopted specifically to accommodate the
listing. S&P Global declined to make an equivalent change to its S&P 500 inclusion
criteria. The result is that the same company will be present in some index products and
absent from others for an extended period, not because of any difference in the
company's underlying characteristics, but because the standards applied by competing
index providers are no longer uniform.
The relevance to this rulemaking is structural, not company-specific. Index providers are
already operating under divergent eligibility standards in response to commercial and
political pressure. The Commission's proposal would layer a second dimension of
divergence on top of that: some issuers in a given index will be quarterly filers and some
will be semiannual filers, with no standardized disclosure to index-fund investors about
which holdings operate under which framework. A retail investor holding an ETF that
tracks an index containing both quarterly and semiannual filers has no reliable basis for
assessing the currency of the financial information underlying their exposure to each
constituent. They are not equipped to investigate the cover page of each holding's most
recent Form 10-K to make that determination.
The Commission's disclosure regime is supposed to create a standardized information
environment that allows retail investors to participate in public markets on terms that do
not require the analytical infrastructure of institutional allocators. When index providers
fragment their eligibility standards and the Commission simultaneously fragments its
disclosure cadence requirements, the cumulative effect is a public market that is
 increasingly legible only to participants with the resources to navigate the divergence.
That is not a stronger market. It is a less public one.
The Commission should consider whether the semiannual reporting proposal, in the
current index market environment, requires affirmative standardization measures,
including prominent, machine-readable disclosure of reporting frequency at the index
constituent level, before any rule reducing disclosure uniformity is finalized.

IX. Conclusion
I urge the Commission not to adopt the proposed rule in its current form. If the
Commission concludes that some form of reduced-frequency reporting is appropriate, I
recommend the following modifications be considered:
First, eligibility for semiannual reporting should be conditioned on the absence of retail
investor concentration: For example, limited to issuers with institutional ownership
above a defined threshold, or to issuers with market capitalizations above a level
consistent with robust analyst coverage.
Second, any earnings releases issued in lieu of Form 10-Q filings by semiannual filers
should be required to be filed rather than merely furnished, and should be subject to the
same accountant review requirements that currently apply to Form 10-Q interim
financial statements.
Third, the Commission should require disclosure in both the election mechanism on
Form 10-K and in any investor-facing communication that the company has elected
semiannual reporting and that interim financial information will not be subject to
mandatory quarterly filing requirements during the applicable fiscal year.
The Commission's mandate includes investor protection. That mandate is not satisfied
by characterizing reduced disclosure as flexibility, or by treating voluntary furnished
practice as equivalent to mandatory filed obligation. I respectfully ask the Commission
to address these concerns before proceeding to a final rule.


Respectfully submitted,




Tracy Ayers, CPA
Cerex Advisory

1853:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Timothy M. Hayes

        I write to inform the Staff of my opposition to the Commission’s proposal to permit reporting companies to cease quarterly
reporting, as described below. All investors, including those who typically do not trade securities in the short term, benefit from being
able to view company results from mostly contemporaneous reporting periods. Under current rules, Forms 10-Q are typically
available about 30 days after a reporting period; this misguided proposal would create a twice-annually information gap period of at
least 90 days.

        I am a (happily) retired former corporate counsel whose clients included reporting companies. Now, as an investor, I and all
other investors benefit from quarterly reporting to enable investors to make informed investment decisions. For example, I routinely
review Management’s Discussion & Analysis especially in times of market upheaval. The most recent MD&A is also useful for
assessing liquidity and capital when I consider investing in or disposing of fixed income securities.

       While the advocates for this proposed rule argue that it will reduce expenses and create focus on longer-term investment
horizons, I have a vastly different view:

           •   The United States has a well-deserved, stellar reputation for open and fair securities markets with robust disclosure
               framework, which will be eroded as reporting companies are tempted to save a few basis points in an expense line-item
               by delaying a periodic report.
           •   Investors will be “in the dark” about important company developments for extended periods of time when quarterly
               reports are missing.
           •   Rumors, speculation, and innuendo from unscrupulous secondary market traders can fill an information void in the
               absence of accurate, timely information, thus emboldening speculators and actually leading to greater market volatility
               in the short term.
           •   Management presentations filling a 10-Q reporting void may become ‘pitch’ documents not presenting a complete
               picture for an investor to be able to form an informed view about a reporting company.
           •   Over time, investors will begin to view U.S. securities markets as tilted in favor of insiders and those who have special
               access to information that the public doesn’t have.

       This ill-advised proposal is a solution in search of a nonexistent problem, and would result in less transparency and greater
       market volatility. I urge the Commission to reject this intuitive.

       Respectfully,

       Timothy M. Hayes

From: Abigail Rider

I am Abigail Rider, a recently retired CFO of a flagship state university. The plurality of the US stock market, about $49 trillion, is owned by retirement funds (such as 401(k)s, IRAs, and defined-benefit pension plans) managing retail investors’ assets. Retirement funds need to be able to evaluate their holdings on a timely basis in order to maintain the confidence of the millions of investors they represent. The loss of quarterly information would make the entire system more vulnerable to panics since the funds couldn’t evaluate their holdings frequently and relate their performance to the way the market is performing. Especially today when insider dealing has risen to a shocking degree, people need to know that their retirement assets are being continuously and rigorously evaluated and kept safe. Quarterly reporting ensures that this is the case. The argument that quarterly reporting makes companies avoid long term capital investment because it drags quarterly earnings down is specious - capital spending does not run through the P&L except as a provision after operating profit, and therefore does not depress operating earnings. It only reduces only the amount available for dividends to the shareholders and presumably the shareholders who hold the stock are on board with management’s growth plans. It’s the corporate officers who don’t want to have to worry about quarterly operating earnings, but that’s their job and they are paid huge salaries to do so. It is critical to maintain retail investor confidence in the market. Do not remove the quarterly reporting requirement. Thank you.

1891:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: MK CFA

The proposed change eliminating quarterly reporting requirements for public companies is inexcusable-  I can not think of a single reason to relax reporting requirements which are crucial to public trust and market efficiency. 

If the argument is that quarterly reporting encourages short-term decision-making or that quarterly reporting is too onerous, that doesn't hold up. Executives are always supposed to act in the best interest of shareholders, and there's no reason why quarterly reporting would lead an ethical executive to act any differently- if the issue is ethics, that's not something that can or should be addressed with reporting frequency. As far as level of effort, staying with the status quo does not cause any increase in work. 

As far as market efficiency is concerned, there is a very real and significant risk for relaxing reporting requirements. Markets are most efficient when they incorporate a greater volume and greater accuracy of information. By eliminating quarterly reporting, the SEC is proposing restricting both the volume and accuracy of information available to the public. This means that investors of all categories are flying blind and cannot access the information they require to inform their investment decision-making.  This hurts pensions, retirees, and nearly everyone who participates in US equity investing. 

In addition, requiring less accountability through less frequent reporting also creates a risk of unethical behavior because the public will have less visibility into the activities of publicly traded companies. Circling back to my point from earlier, this proposed change creates a larger, not smaller risk of unethical behavior. You can't control the number of unethical executives out there with this kind of change, but you can control how they are monitored. 

Sometimes I feel like the world is going insane and I cannot do anything to make an impact, but I had the write this comment anyway. 

MK CFA

1920:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Lesli Hogan

Affiliation: 

Jun. 7, 2026

 
The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) utilizes a standard 30- to 60-day notice-and-comment process to evaluate its rules. While the SEC occasionally explores altering these timeframes for specific rulemakings, advocates, industry panels, and lawmakers consistently push back against proposals that cut or shorten this period for several key reasons: 



 Inadequate Time for Deep Analysis: Complex financial regulations require significant time for institutional investors, analysts, and public interest groups to model economic impacts, compliance burdens, and risks. Shortened periods hamstring the SEC from gathering the expert feedback needed to craft surgical, effective proposals. 


 Disenfranchisement of Retail Investors: Shorter comment windows disproportionately harm individual and retail investors who lack dedicated legal and regulatory teams. They require additional time to review proposals and draft their perspectives. 


 Skewed Holiday Timelines: Comment periods that are hastily scheduled or overlap with major holiday seasons (such as Thanksgiving or Christmas) make it difficult for working professionals, nonprofits, and smaller entities to participate meaningfully. 


 Increased Risk of Unintended Consequences: Rushed rulemaking—whether it involves rescinding disclosures or changing reporting frequencies—can lead to poor oversight, "information asymmetry," and increased susceptibility to corporate fraud. 


 Loss of Diverse Perspectives: When comment periods are cut, rulemaking often becomes dominated by heavily resourced corporate lobbyists, which can erode trust in the perceived fairness of the regulatory process. 

Sources, Wall Street Journal, Cato Institute
 


Lesli Hogan

1989:
California Public Employees' Retirement System
Executive Office

Vanessa A. Countryman, Secretary
Securities and Exchange Commission
100 F Street, NE
Washington, DC 20549-1090

June 19, 2026

Subject: Proposed Rule on Semiannual Reporting, File No. S7-2026-15

Dear Secretary Countryman,

On behalf of the California Public Employees' Retirement System (CalPERS), we appreciate the opportunity to provide comments on the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission's (SEC or Commission) proposed rule to allow companies to file semiannual reports on the new Form 10-S in lieu of quarterly reports on Form 10-Q.

As the largest public defined benefit pension fund in the United States, CalPERS manages approximately $600 billion in global assets on behalf of nearly 2.4 million members. We seek long-term and sustainable returns through efficient capital allocation and stewardship in line with our fiduciary duty.

CalPERS respectfully but strongly urges the Commission to withdraw the proposed amendments and retain its existing quarterly reporting framework for all domestic registrants. The current periodic reporting cadence, which has been in place since 1970, is foundational to the depth, liquidity, and integrity of U.S. public markets and is directly relied upon by CalPERS and many similarly situated long-term investors to fulfill our fiduciary duties. Reducing the frequency of mandatory periodic reporting will not, in our view, meaningfully advance the Commission's stated objective of encouraging companies to go and stay public. Instead, we believe it will reduce transparency, widen information asymmetry, and weaken accountability between management and shareholders.

If the Commission's objective is to lengthen corporate decision-making horizons, then the more effective and well-targeted lever, as we have urged in prior comment letters, is to discourage the voluntary issuance of forward quarterly earnings guidance, not to dilute the historical financial reporting on which investors depend.

CalPERS has long believed that timely, comparable, and decision-useful financial disclosure is the bedrock of the U.S. capital markets. In our 2019 comment letter responding to the Commission's request for comment on earnings releases and quarterly reports (File No. S7-26-18), CalPERS provided "a strong endorsement for the existing framework of quarterly and annual reporting," and we reaffirm that position today.

Quarterly reporting performs several functions that semiannual reporting cannot replicate:

- Quarterly reporting reduces information asymmetry. Mandatory quarterly disclosure ensures material financial information reaches all investors on the same timely cadence, narrowing the gap between informed insiders and the broader investing public. Doubling the interval between reports widens that gap and increases the likelihood that markets price securities on selective disclosure, rumor, or alternative-data signals available only to a subset of participants.

- Quarterly reporting enables trend analysis and risk monitoring. Long-term investors, like CalPERS, rely on quarterly data to observe operating trends and identify emerging risks. Four data points per year materially improve trend identification compared with two; semiannual data is too coarse to detect inflection points in revenue, margins, working capital, or leverage before they become material to valuation.

- Quarterly reporting mitigates a dangerous gap in semiannual reporting. Proponents of the proposed amendments often point to foreign jurisdictions—such as the United Kingdom, Australia, or the European Union—as successful models of semiannual reporting. However, this comparison relies on a fundamentally flawed equivalence of underlying legal frameworks. Those markets pair semiannual periodic reporting with principles-based continuous disclosure requirements that mandate the immediate public release of any material information. In contrast, the U.S. disclosure requirements rely heavily on the specific, itemized triggers of Form 8-K for intra-period updates. Superimposing a semiannual cadence onto the existing U.S. framework is likely to create a significant disclosure loophole. Under the proposed rule, management could legally embargo material but non-8-K-triggering developments—such as severe margin compression, deteriorating sales trends, or shifting competitive dynamics—for up to six months. Depriving the market of this information until the next Form 10-S filing is directly contrary to the Commission's mandate to protect investors and maintain fair, orderly, and efficient markets.

- Quarterly reporting prevents the erosion of peer comparability. Because the SEC's proposal allows companies to elect their reporting frequency annually, it is likely to severely fragment the disclosure landscape. If some companies in a certain sector continue reporting quarterly while others shift to semiannual reporting, investors will lose the ability to accurately benchmark performance, compare relative risk, and perform fundamental analysis across peers.

- Quarterly reporting supports fiduciary duty. CalPERS has a fiduciary obligation to administer assets prudently on behalf of beneficiaries, which requires the most timely and accurate financial information reasonably available. We believe a disclosure framework permitting large public companies to go up to nine months between full periodic reports is incompatible with that standard.

- Quarterly reporting supports proxy voting and active ownership. Most U.S. public companies hold their Annual General Meetings in the spring. Under a quarterly system, Q1 data is generally available to inform proxy voting decisions. Under a semiannual system, investors would routinely be forced to vote on director elections, executive compensation (Say-on-Pay), and critical shareholder proposals based on stale, year-end data that is five or six months old. Semiannual reporting could impair shareholder oversight.

- Quarterly reporting deters fraud and supports internal control integrity. More frequent reporting creates more frequent reconciliation cycles, auditor reviews, and opportunities for management, audit committees, and external auditors to identify errors and control deficiencies. Reducing these checkpoints risks longer periods during which fraud or earnings management goes undetected.

A longer-term focus on curbing forward quarterly earnings guidance would be more effective than reducing periodic reporting. To the extent the Commission's motivation is to encourage longer-term decision-making by issuers, the most direct and effective lever, as both CalPERS noted in its 2019 comment letter and the Council of Institutional Investors urged in its parallel submission that year, is to discourage the voluntary issuance of forward quarterly earnings guidance, not to weaken the historical reporting that investors rely on.

Forward quarterly earnings guidance (not historical quarterly reporting) is the practice most credibly associated with managerial focus on near-term earnings targets, with the documented "meet-or-beat" dynamics that distort accruals, with deferred research and development outlays, and with end-of-quarter discounting and channel-stuffing. Historical periodic reporting tells investors what happened in the past quarter whereas forward guidance commits management publicly to a near-term earnings outcome and shapes operating decisions accordingly.

In conclusion, CalPERS respectfully urges the Commission to withdraw the proposed amendments and to retain its existing quarterly periodic reporting framework on Form 10-Q. The case for reducing reporting frequency is not supported by the available evidence; the costs to investor protection and market liquidity are real and material; and the underlying objective of encouraging long-term corporate decision-making is more effectively pursued through measures directed at voluntary forward earnings guidance rather than at historical financial reporting.

Thank you for the opportunity to share our comments.

Sincerely,
Marcie Frost
Chief Executive Officer

1990:
June 18, 2026

Via Email: rule-comments@sec.gov

Vanessa Countryman

Secretary

Securities and Exchange Commission
100 F Street, NE

Washington, D.C. 20549-1090

RE: Semiannual Reporting, File No. S7-2026-15

Dear Ms. Countryman:

am writing on behalf of Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. (“Gallagher,” “we” or the “Company”) to comment on the
Commission's recent rule proposal on semiannual reporting. We appreciate the Commission's efforts to streamline
the disclosure process, and we thank you for the opportunity to provide our input.

1. We Support Semiannual But Would Also Support “Triannual” Reporting

We fully support the Commission's proposal to reduce reporting frequency; however, the public debate surrounding
this issue appears to be limited to only two options: quarterly and semiannual. While many long-term investors
support a move to semiannual reporting, we have seen press reports of certain investors expressing reservations,
and many investors expressed concern when the Commission solicited comment on this idea in 2018. We believe
there is a third alternative that also merits consideration, which may prove satisfactory to investors concerned about
a move to semiannual reporting — a "triannual" reporting framework.

In our view, triannual reporting —i.e., reporting every four months instead of every three months — would
meaningfully reduce the burden on companies and provide a reasonable compromise for investors concerned about
a move to semiannual reporting. A triannual framework would simply add one month to each reporting period. This
middle ground approach could drive material cost savings through the elimination of a full quarterly reporting cycle
and support a reduction in potential managerial short-termism while maintaining a regular cadence of material
disclosure for investor benefit. Moreover, triannual reporting may strike a better balance between companies’
interests and investors’ interests; and if it is widely adopted, it would promote better comparability among
companies as compared to optional semiannual reporting.

Our support of a reduction in reporting frequency should not be misunderstood as opposition to transparency for
investors. An illustration of this point relates to our "quiet periods" around earnings releases. During quiet periods,
which generally last for a month, our interactions with current and prospective investors are restricted. A reduction in
reporting frequency would reduce the number of quiet periods and create an opportunity for more continuous
dialogue with investors regarding our business strategy. If even one quiet period was eliminated per year, this would
open up significant additional time for us to interact with the investment community.

Therefore, we believe a move to triannual reporting is a strong middle ground opportunity that could alleviate the
burden on reporting companies while also assuaging investors’ and other stakeholders’ concerns about losing
access to the material, periodic information provided in the current quarterly reporting regime. To implement this
idea, the Commission could rename the proposed Form 10-S as “Form 10-T.”

 
 

2. Optionality Reduces the Potential Usefulness of Reduced Reporting Frequency

We believe that mandating a reduction in reporting frequency, rather than making it optional, would more
meaningfully reduce the reporting burden for issuers. Without a mandate, the move from quarterly to a less-frequent
cadence presents a collective action problem. Within industry groups, we believe there will be understandable
pressure from investors for uniformity in reporting frequency. We expect many issuers that would benefit from a
reduction in reporting frequency, and whose investors would support such a move, will be unduly influenced to
continue reporting on a quarterly cadence to match the frequency of their peer companies who may have their own
particular reasons for reporting on a quarterly cadence. Moreover, investors themselves can benefit from less
frequent reporting, as it would afford them additional quality time to more thoroughly analyze and digest the
information contained in each report rather than reacting to a continuous stream of quarterly disclosures.

In a mandatory semiannual or triannual reporting framework, issuers that wish to report their financial results to their
investors more frequently will still be able to do so via interim earnings releases.

Accordingly, we urge the Commission to consider removing optionality as a feature of its proposed rules.
3. Cooling-Off Periods Should Be Adjusted to Align with Reduced Reporting Frequency

Another relevant concern of our company pertains to the usage of 10b5-1 plans in connection with reduced reporting
frequency. We respectfully recommend that, in connection with any final rule, the Commission revisit Rule 10b5-1(c)
to eliminate or substantially streamline the mandatory cooling-off periods applicable to trading plans. This
targeted reform would complement a semiannual or triannual reporting regime and would better align insider trading
safeguards with modern disclosure practices.

In 2022, the Commission amended Exchange Act Rule 10b5-1 to introduce mandatory cooling-off periods (up to 90—
120 days for officers and directors) ostensibly to mitigate opportunistic plan adoption while in possession of material
nonpublic information (“MNPI”). While those requirements were calibrated in the context of a quarterly reporting
cadence, they risk producing unintended and disproportionate constraints if issuers transition to semiannual
reporting. Under a semiannual reporting schedule, issuers that adopt semiannual reporting will likely be required to
implement longer blackout periods around the preparation of interim and annual reports due to the aggregation of
financial results over a longer period and the increased significance of each disclosure event. These longer blackout
windows, combined with existing cooling-off periods, could materially delay insiders’ ability to establish or modify
trading plans, in many cases effectively precluding plan adoption and insider sales for extended portions of the year.
The result would be to undermine the central policy objective of Rule 10b5-1: providing a reliable mechanism for
insiders to achieve liquidity and diversification.

In a semiannual or triannual reporting regime, the marginal benefit of mandatory cooling-off periods becomes
diminished by other practical constraints. First, blackout periods themselves function as de facto cooling periods
restricting plan adoption to windows following broad public disclosure. These blackout periods may become longer
under a semiannual or triannual reporting regime. Second, board-approved insider trading policies, pre-clearance
procedures, and compliance oversight already govern plan adoption and modifications to ensure trading plans are
not effected during periods where MNPI is undisclosed. Finally, Rule 10b5-1 continues to require that plans be
adopted in good faith and not as part of a scheme to evade insider trading prohibitions, with enhanced SEC
enforcement tools and disclosure requirements providing additional deterrence. Accordingly, the incremental investor
protection provided by a mandatory cooling-off period is limited, particularly when balanced against the significant
practical restraints presented by longer trading blackout periods.

 
 

We therefore recommend that the Commission eliminate the mandatory cooling-off periods in Rule 10b5-1(c)(1)(ii);
or, in the alternative, replace the current fixed cooling-off requirements with a principles-based standard tied to issuer
trading windows, such as permitting plan adoption only during an open trading window following public disclosure of
interim period financial results.

This approach would preserve appropriate safeguards while restoring the practical utility of Rule 10b5-1 plans. It
would also harmonize the rule with the Commission's broader objective, reflected in the semiannual rule proposal, of
reducing unnecessary procedural burdens that do not meaningfully advance investor protection.

x ek

In summary, we respectfully urge the Commission to (i) remove optionality as a feature of its proposed semiannual
reporting framework, which we fully support, with mandatory “triannual” reporting as an acceptable alternative
providing a middle ground between the current quarterly regime and the proposed semiannual framework, and (ii) in
connection therewith, modernize Rule 10b5-1(c) by eliminating or streamlining its mandatory cooling-off periods.

We appreciate the opportunity to comment on this important initiative and commend the Commission for undertaking
a review of its disclosure framework. We are available to discuss these matters further at the Commission's
convenience. If you have any questions, or wish to set up a meeting to discuss our ideas further, please contact Sara
Walsh, our Treasurer and Vice President — Finance & Investor Relations, at investor relations@ajg.com.

Respectfully submitted,

t

Douglas K. Howell
Chief Financial Officer
Arthur J. Gallagher & Co.
1991:
June 18, 2026

Vanessa A. Countryman, Secretary
Securities and Exchange Commission
100 F Street NE
Washington, DC 20549-109

Dear Ms. Countryman:

RE: Reforming Proposed Amendments to Permit Optional Semiannual Reporting by Public Companies - File Number S7-2026-15

Thank you for the opportunity to comment on the Securities and Exchange Commission's proposal to permit domestic reporting companies to file one semiannual report on Form 10-S and one annual report per fiscal year, in lieu of quarterly reports on Form 10-Q. I am writing on behalf of Calcbench, a financial data platform used by institutional and individual investors, financial advisors, and fundamental research analysts.

Our firm strongly opposes moving away from quarterly reporting. If enacted, it will increase the costs to ALL investors. It will drive up transaction costs and limit price discovery. It will hurt exactly those investors that the SEC protects.

The Case for Change

The premise for allowing companies to report semi-annually rests primarily on reducing the regulatory and compliance burden of being a public company — lowering costs, enabling executives to focus on long-term strategic execution rather than short-term earnings pressure, and motivating more companies to go or remain public without undermining fundamental investor protections.

Proponents point to markets across the globe that have already made this shift, including the European Union and the United Kingdom, and countries that have always had a semi-annual reporting cadence, including Japan and Australia. They also point to a notable chorus of business leaders supporting the change, from Jamie Dimon of JPMorgan Chase to Kunal Kapoor of Morningstar, Adena Friedman of Nasdaq, and the Business Roundtable, which represents approximately 200 CEOs of leading U.S. companies.

The Stronger Case for Quarterly Reporting

We strongly believe that these proposed changes have the potential to compromise the shareholders these companies ultimately serve. The CFA Institute surveyed 2,500 members working as investment analysts and portfolio managers as part of its recent report, Investor Perspectives: Quarterly Reporting – What Investors Tell Us About Quarterly Reporting, Why It Matters and Why They Support It in an Era of Artificial Intelligence. These members strongly supported retaining mandatory quarterly reporting. In addition, prominent asset managers and quantitative investment firms — including Citadel, Fidelity, Two Sigma, Blackrock, T.Rowe Price, and D.E. Shaw — have all warned against the move.

Shareholders want more information, not less. Reduced reporting frequency will increase market price volatility, increase transaction costs, and diminish the ability to monitor company performance. Overall transparency won't increase — which is precisely why India and China, the two largest emerging markets, have moved toward quarterly reporting over the last several decades.

The demand for more data, not less, is not theoretical. Calcbench clients collectively manage more than $20 trillion in assets under management, and our direct experience working with these institutions reveals how professional investors actually use quarterly financial disclosures. In a cohort of our institutional investors, data usage increased 20.3% year over year — from 285.5K to 343.5K queries to our database. That is a clear signal of growing reliance on data, not diminishing need.

That reliance runs deep. Institutional investors have built their analytical frameworks, risk models, and portfolio monitoring processes around the cadence of quarterly disclosure. A shift to semiannual reporting wouldn't just reduce the frequency of required filings; it would create extended periods when professional investors — and ultimately their retail clients — must make capital allocation decisions with materially less information about the companies they hold or are evaluating.

The information gap would not be filled equally. Institutional investors may be able to request data directly from company investor relations teams (which, incidentally, raises the risk of Regulation FD violations) — but retail investors have no such access. As for the claim that Form 8-K filings would bridge the gap: an 8-K can signal that something material has happened, but it does not translate that event into its income statement or balance sheet impact. That financial translation is precisely what quarterly reporting provides.

Nor do we believe that these changes will help the IPO market. As Shivaram Rajgopal, the Roy Bernard Kester and T.W. Byrnes Professor of Accounting and Auditing at Columbia Business School, has stated, "The defining feature of an IPO is information asymmetry — management knows the business; the public doesn't yet. Quarterly reporting is one of the fastest mechanisms to close that gap. Reducing it makes IPO investing more opaque, not less risky to avoid."

Moreover, IPOs are already making a comeback with quarterly reporting intact. The Financial Times reported that "sixty US companies have gone public this year, raising nearly $40bn, the highest year-to-date deal value since 2021, according to data from Dealogic that excludes listings of blank-cheque companies. Goldman expects that figure to rise to a record $225bn this year following the raft of big listings."

The cost of the regulatory and compliance "burden" on public companies is the "cost" component of running a vibrant equity market. Authors of the above mentioned CFA Institute report, Sandy Peters and Matthew Winters, highlight that the supposed costs for quarterly reporting "represents but a tiny fraction (.004%) of the approximately $67 trillion in equity market capitalization of the NYSE and NASDAQ; and the SEC's reporting framework established under the Securities Acts of 1933 and 1934 has been a major contributor to the investor confidence underlying these markets."

Lastly, we do not believe that providing the option for companies to report semiannually will reduce a company's "short-termism" and give executives the time and space to focus on long-term investments. As we've seen this year, thanks in part to AI, companies have moved away from short-termism and are investing for the long-term. Based on our recent earnings tracker, which looked at roughly 3,600 companies from Q1 2025 to Q1 2026, we found that net capital expenditures are up 29.5% this year over last; companies are already making plans across long time horizons.

In Conclusion

While Calcbench appreciates the Commission's effort to encourage more companies to access public capital markets, we urge the Commission not to proceed with optional semiannual reporting. Our opposition to this proposal stems from concerns about investor protection and our proprietary data, which shows demonstrated and growing demand for high-frequency financial data among the sophisticated market participants who rely on quarterly disclosures as a core input to their investment processes. We strongly believe these proposed amendments will be bad for large financial institutions and for small investors: friends, neighbors, and fellow Americans. Both directly and indirectly, costs on these people will increase.

If the goal is to reduce regulatory burden, streamlining the 10-Q is a more logical path. If the goal is long-term investment, the focus should be on executive compensation structures. But eliminating quarterly reporting is not a solution to any of the problems its proponents describe.

Sincerely,
Pranav Ghai
Co-Founder and CEO, Calcbench

218:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Jennifer Simpson

Hello SEC:
Here are four reasons why Form 10-s is bad for every investor:
1. You'd be flying blind for six months -- A lot can go wrong in half a year. Failed product launches, ballooning debt, lawsuits, vanishing customers. Today the law forces companies to surface that information every quarter. Under the new rule, they can sit on it for six months at a stretch. Plenty of time for insiders to quietly sell their shares to you (or your ETF) before the bad news tanks the stock.
2. It allows more time for fraud to grow -- Enron. WorldCom. Madoff. The run-up to 2008. Every major modern accounting scandal was enabled by gaps and opacity in financial disclosure. Quarterly filings don't prevent fraud, but they dramatically shorten the window in which it can fester before someone outside the company can see it. What if Enron only had to cook their books twice a year?
3. Be not fooled, "Optional" is a trick -- The framing is voluntary, but the dynamics aren't. Once large companies start filing twice a year, smaller competitors face pressure to follow. They can't justify "more compliance than the bigger guy."" Investor expectations adjust downward. What started as a choice quietly becomes the new normal. Almost no rule like this stays optional for long.
4. The supposed savings are a miniscule, and they don't go to you -- Companies still have to keep their books. They still have to brief their boards. The actual savings from skipping a public filing are modest legal and accounting fees, and those savings flow to the company and its executives. The cost of worse information falls on you, the person whose 401(k), IRA, or college fund holds the stock. That's a bad trade for ordinary investors.
Remember, it's "We the People" in America, not "We the Corporations".

Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Kevin W. Alexander

Affiliation: 

May 18, 2026

 
The SEC's proposed plan to provide public companies the option to NOT file quarterly reports, and instead to file only semi-annual reports, is a terrible idea. 


Public investors absolutely need the information published quarterly in order to make informed investment decisions. Visibility 4 times a year allows for necessary price / value adjustments and is highly efficient. 


The time commitment of quarterly reporting for public companies is minimal, when compared to the vast benefits companies gain when they can access the world's leading public markets in the US. 


Most importantly, investors in US markets deeply depend on quarterly financial reporting, which is one of the main reasons our markets are far and away the largest, most liquid and most attractive in the world. If the US converts to semi-annual reporting, expect to see US markets become weaker, smaller and less liquid like those that report semi-annually. Look at European markets for the results that will follow if the SEC converts to this proposed rule change. 


Finally, keep in mind that financial markets are a competitive business. Other lower tier markets (London, Hong Kong, etc.) will certainly gain and become more attractive alternatives if the US moves to semi-annual reporting. If the concern is that US markets need more of our private companies graduating to public companies (something I agree with), then focus on promoting other incentives and benefits for these private companies -- not on reduced financial reporting. No one seriously believes that the litany of private tech (and other) companies are choosing to stay private longer b/c they don't want to report quarterly. 


Please don't adopt this ill-informed rule change. 


Sincerely, 


Kevin W. Alexander

257:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Lance Graven

Subject: File Number S7-2026-15 - Opposition to Proposed Rule Change for Semiannual Reporting
Dear Secretary Countryman,
I am writing to express my strong opposition to the Securities and Exchange Commission's proposal to permit public companies to transition from quarterly financial reporting to semiannual reporting. While I understand the stated goal is to reduce corporate compliance costs and alleviate short-term market pressures, I believe this change will severely harm individual investors, degrade market transparency, and increase financial volatility.
Quarterly reporting is a cornerstone of the modern, transparent U.S. capital markets. Moving to a six-month reporting cycle introduces several significant risks:
Increased Information Asymmetry: Individual retail investors rely heavily on timely, standardized financial disclosures to make informed investment decisions. Extending the reporting window to six months creates a massive information vacuum. This gap will disproportionately benefit institutional investors who have the resources to utilize alternative data streams, leaving retail investors at a severe disadvantage.
Heightened Market Volatility: Waiting six months to receive audited financial updates means that unexpected operational shifts, supply chain disruptions, or macroeconomic impacts will build up behind closed doors. When these results are finally released, the market reactions are likely to be far more volatile and disruptive than the gradual adjustments seen under a quarterly system.
Reduced Management Accountability: Quarterly oversight forces corporate executives to maintain strict financial discipline and operational accountability throughout the fiscal year. A semiannual framework reduces this regular check-and-balance, potentially allowing corporate inefficiencies or strategic missteps to go unnoticed by the public for too long.
Ineffectiveness of Voluntary Updates: Allowing companies to provide informal interim updates via Form 8-K is an inadequate substitute for standardized Form 10-Q filings. Fragmented, non-standardized disclosures make it difficult for investors to accurately compare performance across companies or sectors.
For these reasons, I urge the Commission to withdraw this proposal and maintain the current quarterly reporting framework. Protecting the integrity, transparency, and fairness of our markets for all participants must remain the SEC's highest priority.
Thank you for your time and consideration of my comments.
Sincerely,
 Lance Graven

259:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Rei Takashi

Dear Secretary,

I am writing to formally log my opposition to the Commission's rule proposal to permit public issuers the option to transition from quarterly financial reporting to a semiannual reporting framework on Form 10-S. 

While acknowledging the Commission's intent to mitigate short-termism and lower corporate compliance costs, empirical evidence and economic principles suggest this framework will degrade market efficiency, elevate cost of capital, and exacerbate information asymmetry.

The introduction of a semiannual reporting cycle creates systemic risks across several critical vectors:

Information Asymmetry and Bid-Ask Spreads: In the fields of financial economics and market microstructure, reducing reporting frequency directly increases information asymmetry. A six-month reporting gap creates an information vacuum. Institutional market participants possess the capital to acquire proprietary, alternative data streams to bridge this gap, whereas retail investors do not. Consequently, market makers will likely widen bid-ask spreads to protect against adverse selection, increasing transaction costs and reducing liquidity for the broader market.

Delayed Price Discovery and Volatility Clustering: Quarterly reporting facilitates continuous, incremental price discovery. Extending the reporting interval to six months will cause material structural data to compound behind a curtain of corporate confidentiality. Consequently, when earnings are finally disclosed, the price adjustment process will be discrete rather than continuous. This will result in heightened idiosyncratic volatility and severe price gaps upon announcement, destabilizing secondary market trading.

Insufficiency of Form 8-K as an Interim Substitute: The proposal relies on voluntary, interim financial disclosures furnished via Form 8-K to maintain market communication. This mechanism is structurally flawed. Disclosures under Item 2.02 or 7.01 lack the standardized presentation, mandatory GAAP reconciliations, and strict disclosure controls and procedures (DCP) inherent to Form 10-Q. Relying on fragmented, unstandardized updates diminishes comparability across peers and increases the cognitive processing burden on market analysts.

Agency Costs and Corporate Governance: Quarterly reporting acts as a vital commitment mechanism that aligns manager and shareholder incentives. Extending the reporting window increases the duration of unmonitored management discretion. This lag delays the market's ability to identify capital misallocation, operational drift, or deteriorating internal controls, thereby elevating agency costs and eroding governance discipline.

The proposed reduction in regulatory costs is heavily outweighed by the negative externalities imposed on capital allocation efficiency and market transparency. I strongly encourage the Commission to preserve the current mandated quarterly disclosure regime under Exchange Act Rules 13a-13 and 15d-13.

Thank you for analyzing these technical considerations.
25:
Subject: S7-2026-15: Webform Comments from Isaiah Owolabi

RE: Proposed Amendments to Permit Optional Semiannual Reporting by Public Companies
File No. S7-2026-15
Submitted by: Isaiah Owolabi, LL.M. (FinTech Law & Regulation)
Founder and CEO, ESGine | Originator, ESG-as-Code, Washington, DC

I want to start by saying I think this proposal is a step in the right direction. Quarterly reporting has created a rhythm that works well for some companies and feels like unnecessary overhead for others. Giving companies the flexibility to choose makes sense.

But I want to flag something that I do not think has been addressed clearly enough in the proposal, and it matters a great deal for how companies interpret this change.

Reporting frequency and disclosure obligations are two completely different things. One is about when you file. The other is about what the law requires you to say when you do.

A company that switches from quarterly to semiannual reporting does not reduce what it has to disclose. It simply consolidates those disclosures into fewer filing events. The legal obligations remain exactly the same. The clause does not move. Only the calendar does.

I raise this because I have spent the past several years working at the intersection of law, technology, and ESG compliance. Through ESGine and ESG-as-Code, I have built a platform that evaluates ESG and climate disclosures against specific regulatory clauses across seven major frameworks including SEC Climate, CSRD, CARB SB253, CARB SB261, ISSB, UK FCA, and SFDR. The companies using it are not struggling with how often to report. They are struggling with knowing exactly what each regulatory clause requires and whether their disclosure actually satisfies it.

That is the harder problem. And it does not get easier with semiannual reporting. If anything it gets more concentrated because everything that would have been spread across three quarterly reports now has to land correctly in one semiannual report.

My concern is that some companies will read this proposal as a signal that the regulatory burden is easing. It is not. The burden is being reorganized. That is a meaningful distinction and I think the final rule should say so explicitly.

I would respectfully ask the Commission to include clear language in any final rule stating that the election of semiannual reporting does not reduce, defer, or modify any substantive disclosure obligation under the federal securities laws. Companies need to understand that frequency is a business decision. Compliance is a legal one.

I would also encourage the Commission to use this moment to highlight the importance of systematic, auditable compliance infrastructure. A company that can trace every disclosure to the specific regulatory clause that requires it is a company that can file quarterly, semiannually, or annually with confidence. That traceability is what regulators actually need to see. It is also what ESG-as-Code was built to provide.

I am happy to discuss any of this further if it would be helpful to the Commission staff.

Respectfully submitted,
Isaiah Owolabi, LL.M. (FinTech Law & Regulation)
Founder and CEO, ESGine
Originator, ESG-as-Code
Washington, DC

26:
I respectfully oppose the proposal to reduce the frequency of required reporting for public issuers from quarterly to semi-annual. While I appreciate the objective of easing compliance burdens, I believe the proposal would have several unintended consequences that could ultimately undermine the transparency, efficiency, and integrity of U.S. capital markets, which are currently the world standard.

First, reducing the cadence of financial reporting would materially diminish the timeliness of information available to investors. Quarterly reporting plays a critical role in providing up-to-date insight into an issuer's financial performance, liquidity, and emerging risks. Moving to a semi-annual model would lengthen the information gap between disclosures, increasing uncertainty and potentially impairing investors' ability to make informed decisions. In volatile or rapidly evolving industries this loss of timely visibility could be particularly significant.

Second, the proposal may lead to increased market volatility and information asymmetry. In the absence of regular, structured reporting, market participants may rely more heavily on informal disclosures, selective communications, or non-GAAP metrics, which may not be subject to the same level of rigor or internal control. This dynamic could disproportionately benefit well-connected or institutional investors, to the detriment of broader market fairness.

Third, quarterly reporting serves as a key discipline mechanism for issuers, reinforcing the importance of robust internal controls, close processes, and ongoing financial oversight. Reducing reporting frequency could inadvertently weaken these governance practices, leading to less frequent monitoring of financial trends, delayed identification of issues, and increased risk of error or misstatement accumulating over longer reporting periods.

Additionally, from an audit and assurance perspective, interim reporting supports continuous engagement between auditors and management, facilitating earlier identification and resolution of complex accounting issues. A shift to semi-annual reporting could compress review timelines and increase the complexity and risk associated with interim reviews and annual audits.

Finally, the U.S. reporting framework has long been viewed as a global benchmark for transparency and investor protection. A move away from quarterly reporting could represent a step backward relative to this standard and may negatively impact investor confidence in U.S. capital markets.

For these reasons, I believe the current quarterly reporting framework strikes an appropriate balance between transparency and compliance burden and should be retained. Any efforts to reduce regulatory burden would be better directed toward simplifying disclosures, modernizing reporting requirements, or providing targeted relief for smaller issuers rather than reducing the frequency of financial reporting.

## SUMMARY OF THE LINKED FORBES ARTICLE (summarized for the tracker; not the original text)

Her SEC comment links to her Forbes article "The Hidden Risk of Less Disclosure: Why Banks Need Quarterly Reporting" (2026-05-06). In my own words:

She opposes the optional-semiannual proposal as applied to banks. Banking runs on confidence, so anything that increases opacity is dangerous. The largest banks will stay quarterly; the consequential cohort is mid-sized regional banks (roughly $20-150B in assets), which face real cost pressure and thinner analyst coverage and are the most likely to opt in, and that is exactly where transparency is already most fragile. Under semiannual reporting, gradual credit deterioration and deposit outflows stay invisible until they surface all at once, turning what would have been incremental repricing into a single shock; in a digitally connected system (the 2023 SVB collapse is her reference point) that can trigger rapid deposit flight and contagion to peer institutions, especially other semiannual reporters that investors cannot easily distinguish. She also flags a "signaling trap": once some banks switch, the choice itself signals lower transparency or higher risk, so stronger banks keep quarterly while switchers face higher funding costs, stratifying the system. She urges regulators, investors, credit-rating analysts, and banks to file comments.
296:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Hayden Ramsey

I am writing in support of the Commission's proposed optional semiannual reporting framework. I believe the proposal represents a thoughtful modernization of the public company reporting system and could meaningfully improve operational efficiency, reduce unnecessary compliance burdens, and encourage additional companies to access the public markets.

First, the current quarterly reporting structure imposes substantial administrative and financial burdens on public companies, particularly smaller issuers and emerging growth companies. Public company finance and accounting teams spend significant time each quarter preparing Form 10-Q filings, coordinating external auditors and legal counsel, drafting disclosures, reviewing internal controls, and responding to evolving regulatory expectations. While transparency remains critically important, much of this process is repetitive and resource-intensive relative to the incremental informational value provided every ninety days.

Allowing companies the option to report semiannually would create meaningful efficiencies by enabling management teams to devote more time toward long-term operational execution, strategic planning, capital allocation, and innovation rather than continuous short-cycle reporting preparation. This would be especially impactful for smaller public companies that often operate with lean finance organizations and disproportionately high compliance costs relative to their market capitalization.

Second, the proposal could help reduce barriers to entry for private companies considering becoming publicly listed. The cost and complexity associated with maintaining public company reporting requirements is frequently cited as a deterrent to entering the public markets. Quarterly reporting requirements contribute significantly to those costs through legal fees, accounting fees, audit review procedures, disclosure preparation, investor relations support, and internal staffing demands.

An optional semiannual framework could make public market participation more accessible to growth-stage companies by lowering recurring compliance expenditures and reducing the operational strain associated with quarterly reporting cycles. Over time, this may encourage a broader and healthier public company ecosystem by making the public markets more attractive to innovative companies that might otherwise remain private.

Third, the proposal recognizes that modern markets already operate within an environment of continuous disclosure. Material developments would still require timely reporting through Form 8-K filings, earnings releases, investor presentations, and other public communications. As a result, investors would continue to receive prompt disclosure regarding material events while companies gain flexibility around formal periodic reporting requirements.

Importantly, the proposal is optional rather than mandatory. This preserves market-driven decision-making by allowing companies and investors to determine whether quarterly or semiannual reporting best fits a company's specific circumstances, investor base, and industry dynamics. Companies that believe quarterly reporting provides competitive advantages or better aligns with shareholder expectations would remain free to continue that practice.

In my view, the proposal appropriately balances investor protection with regulatory efficiency and reflects an overdue reassessment of whether the current quarterly reporting structure remains optimally calibrated for today's public markets.

Thank you for considering these comments.

29:
To the Securities and Exchange Commission:

I am writing to oppose the Commission's proposed rule permitting public companies to replace quarterly Form 10-Q reporting with semiannual reporting on new Form 10-S.
I understand the stated goal of reducing compliance burdens and giving companies more flexibility. However, the public markets do not exist only for the convenience of reporting companies. They exist because investors, employees, retirees, pension funds, analysts, and the broader public can rely on timely, standardized, and comparable information. Reducing mandatory reporting from quarterly to semiannual would weaken that foundation.

Quarterly reporting is not just an administrative exercise. It is one of the core transparency mechanisms that allows investors to evaluate company performance, monitor risk, compare companies, and detect changes before they become larger problems. Moving to semiannual reporting would create longer periods where material business trends, financial deterioration, operational risks, liquidity concerns, or governance issues may remain less visible to the public.

This proposal would also increase information asymmetry. Large institutional investors, insiders, sophisticated analysts, and market participants with access to alternative data may still find ways to monitor company performance between reports. Ordinary investors will not have the same access. A system that gives public companies less frequent disclosure obligations risks widening the gap between those with privileged or expensive information channels and those relying on public filings.

I am also concerned that reducing reporting frequency could increase the risk of insider trading or perceived unfairness in the market. When companies go six months between required interim reports, insiders and select market participants may have access to meaningful performance information long before the public does. Even if existing rules still require disclosure of certain material events, semiannual reporting would reduce the regular cadence of structured, comprehensive financial disclosure.

The proposal also raises concerns about comparability. If some companies continue quarterly reporting while others move to semiannual reporting, investors may lose a consistent basis for comparing companies across industries. Optional reporting flexibility may sound reasonable, but in practice it could create a fragmented disclosure landscape where companies with weaker performance have an incentive to disclose less frequently.

The Commission's mission includes protecting investors, maintaining fair and efficient markets, and facilitating capital formation. This proposal may reduce costs for some registrants, but those savings should not come at the expense of transparency, investor protection, market confidence, and fair access to information. For these reasons, I urge the Commission not to adopt the proposed semiannual reporting option. Public companies that access U.S. capital markets should continue to provide quarterly Form 10-Q reports as a baseline obligation of public ownership.

317:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Arnold Bass

I am writing as an individual investor to express my strong opposition to any proposal that would reduce the frequency of corporate earnings reporting from quarterly to twice a year. I believe such a change would meaningfully weaken market transparency, reduce investor confidence, and ultimately harm both retail investors and the broader financial system.

Quarterly reporting is one of the most important mechanisms we have for maintaining timely, accurate insight into a companyâs financial health. These reports allow investors to evaluate performance trends, assess management decisions, and make informed investment choices based on current information rather than outdated data. Moving to semiannual reporting would create longer periods of uncertainty, widen information gaps, and increase the likelihood of market volatility driven by speculation rather than facts.

Less frequent reporting would also disadvantage individual investors compared to institutional investors who have greater access to alternative data sources. Quarterly disclosures help level the playing field by ensuring that all market participants receive standardized, regulated information at predictable intervals.

Furthermore, quarterly reporting promotes accountability. Companies are more likely to maintain disciplined financial practices when they know their performance will be reviewed and disclosed every three months. Reducing this cadence risks weakening oversight and could allow emerging problems to go unnoticed for longer periods.

While I understand the desire to reduce regulatory burdens on public companies, I believe the benefits of quarterly reporting far outweigh the costs. Transparency is the foundation of healthy markets, and any step that reduces it would be a step backward for investors and the public.

Thank you for considering my perspective on this important issue. I urge the Commission to preserve quarterly earnings reporting requirements in the interest of fairness, transparency, and market stability.

I write this as an individual investor and compliance professional that stands strongly against this proposal. Although we see elite investors like Warren Buffett and Jamie Dimon comment how quarterly reporting has "contributed to the decline in the number of public companies in America over the past two decades," of what relevance is this to the individual investor, to which the Commission is responsible for protecting? I don't believe it's any coincidence that the protection of investors is the number one priority listed in the mission statement, and here's a great opportunity to assert that.

Lastly, does the expansion of publicly traded companies make up for the possibility of an Enron-like disaster repeating? Is $60B in investor losses a sufficient tradeoff for more lax reporting standards? Why give publicly traded companies any more opportunity to dupe the individual investor?
